**Lampri et al. (2024) Review**: Published in *Autism Research*, this comprehensive review found that Theory of Mind (ToM) and verbal skills correlated most strongly with figurative language comprehension in ASD, more than executive functions. The review emphasized that differences in research methodology and task properties may have led to significant discrepancies between study results.

**Lim, Young & Brewer (2022)** and surrounding literature.

- Lim, A., Young, R. L., & Brewer, N. (2022). "Autistic Adults May Be Erroneously Perceived as Deceptive and Lacking Credibility." PMC8813809. (The observer effect that creates the pushback paradox: refusing the concern sounds like exactly what a worried person would say about.)
- Lim, Young & Brewer (2022) — Autistic Adults Perceived as Deceptive — [PMC8813809](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8813809/)

- **Lim, Young & Brewer's (2022) credibility discount** documents that when an autistic person pushes back against a non-autistic interlocutor, third-party observers default to believing the interlocutor over the autistic person. When the interlocutor also carries publicly visible allyship credentials, the default is compounded: observers now have a second reason — *they're an ally, so surely they wouldn't* — to disbelieve the autistic person.

Lim, Young & Brewer (2022), in their paper "Autistic Adults May Be Erroneously Perceived as Deceptive and Lacking Credibility," documented that autistic adults are perceived as less credible and more deceptive than non-autistic adults in observer studies, even when the autistic person is telling the truth. The effect is not moral — it operates below conscious awareness and reflects how observers read cues like affect, prosody, gaze, and fluency.
- Lim, Young & Brewer (2022). *JADD* 52(2):490-507. — [PMC8813809](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8813809/)

Lim, Young & Brewer (2022) on the credibility discount applies to social services assessments with particular force. Assessors are making formal evaluative judgments under time pressure, and the features that produce the credibility discount in other contexts produce it in this one too:
- Lim, Young & Brewer (2022). — [PMC8813809](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8813809/)

- Lim, A., Young, R. L., & Brewer, N. (2022). Surrounding empirical work on perceived deception in autistic mock-suspects, including the finding that diagnostic disclosure reduces the credibility discount when received in good faith.
- Lim, A., Young, R. L., & Brewer, N. (2022). Surrounding work on autistic mock-suspects and witness credibility.
- Lim, A., Young, R. L., & Brewer, N. (2022). Surrounding work on perceptions of credibility in mock-suspect studies.
- Lim, A., Young, R. L., & Brewer, N. (2022). Surrounding work on the perception of autistic mock-suspects and witnesses.

bare: - Li, C.N. & Thompson, S.A. (1976). "Subject and Topic: A New Typology of Language." In C. Li (ed.), *Subject and Topic*. Academic Press. [Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Subject-Topic-Charles-N-Li/dp/0124473504)

Locher and Watts (2005) propose **relational work** as the umbrella concept: all the work that participants do to negotiate their relationships in interaction. Politeness is one part of relational work; impoliteness is another part; politic behavior is another; ritualized behavior is another. The analyst's job is to study relational work in its full range, not just the narrow slice that Brown and Levinson focused on.
